channel.title: briefUpdatesMainNLbusinessAndMarkets
channel.description: This is a randomly ordered feed of the most recent entries from briefUpdatesMainNLbusinessAndMarkets, skipping any feeds with errors.

item.title: The secret to all those death-defying Olympic jumps is a giant plastic airbag
item.description: The highlight reel of the Milan Cortina 2026 Winter Olympics was defined by extreme tricks—corkscrews, twists, and flips performed by snowboarders and...

item.title: Burger King wants you to call its president to complain. No, really
item.description: I called Burger King president Tom Curtis a few times this week, but it went straight to voicemail. You can try too, his number is (305) 874-0520. Oka...

item.title: This Olympic skill can boost your job performance
item.description: Olympians aren’t just physically exceptional—they’re masters at managing where their attention and energy go. Cognitive research finds a key link betw...

item.title: Why did the U.S. government sterilize thousands of Native American women in the 1970s? New Mexico is investigating
item.description: “It’s important for New Mexico to understand the atrocities that took place within the borders of our state,” said state Sen. Linda Lopez.

item.title: OpenAI’s Leadership Drama Reveals 5 Hard Truths About Running a High-Growth Company
item.description: When innovation moves at warp speed, leadership gets stress-tested in ways most companies never experience.

item.title: After United flights roll off tarmac, lose tires, audit finds FAA isn’t staffed enough to do regular inspections
item.description: “United has long advocated in favor of providing the FAA with the resources it needs for its important work," the carrier said.

item.title: Jeff Bezos Reveals the Brutal Fundraising Fight Behind Amazon’s Origin Story
item.description: The Amazon founder reflected on a mistake he made at the very beginning.

item.title: PacifiCorp settles wildfire claims for over half a billion dollars
item.description: The settlement resolves claims that electrical lines negligently started fires in Oregon in 2020 and in California in 2020 and 2022, the DOJ said.

item.title: Utah mother who self-published a children’s book about husband’s death now on trial for his murder
item.description: Kouri Richins, 35, faces nearly counts including aggravated murder, attempted murder, forgery, mortgage fraud and insurance fraud.

item.title: Reagan Economist Sounds the Alarm: AI Is ‘Overhyped and Potentially Dangerous’
item.description: Market expert Steve Hanke predicts AI bubble will burst if Big Tech fails to meet its “spectacular” revenue forecasts.

item.title: How to prepare for a market crash
item.description: How are you feeling about the stock market these days? Depending on who you talk to, the American economy is either poised to soar into the stratosphe...

item.title: ‘Zizian’ cult member marked by beliefs about veganism, animal rights, gender identity and AI bailed out of jail
item.description: Police in Maryland connected Jack “Ziz” LaSota, Michelle Zajko and Daniel Blank to homicide investigations, according to court documents.

item.title: Why using facial recognition on your phone could leave you vulnerable
item.description: Biometric authentication—the ability to unlock your devices by using just your face or fingerprint—is one of the few smartphone features that, even to...

item.title: 3 Ways to Build Rapport and Understanding Before You Negotiate
item.description: When you learn how to connect with people, you will be able to better understand, collaborate, and negotiate with them.

item.title: AI Can Talk You Out of Being Right. New Research Shows How
item.description: When you challenge generative AI, it may not just correct itself but persuade you to override your expertise.

item.title: Why Nvidia is ‘highly motivated’ to keep investing in OpenAI
This once-hot cancer-detection company’s stock fell 50% after a failed trial
Asset managers' stocks fell on private-credit fears. What’s worrying investors.
Trump raises global tariff to 15% in latest response to Supreme Court ruling
Supreme Court tariff ruling has economists even more stressed over the U.S. debt
